items.q: What is the difference between overt and covert prestige?
items.correct: Overt prestige is the high status of standard varieties recognised by society (RP, Standard English); covert prestige is the hidden status of non-standard varieties within particular groups, signalling solidarity or toughness.
items.misconception: Only standard language has prestige; non-standard varieties have none.
items.random_wrong: Overt prestige is loud speech and covert prestige is quiet speech.
items.why: Covert prestige explains why people maintain non-standard varieties despite social stigma — they carry value within their communities. Students assume prestige is single-dimensional, missing why language change isn't all toward the standard.

items.q: What is convergence in accommodation theory?
items.correct: When a speaker adjusts their speech to be more similar to their interlocutor's — to express solidarity, gain approval or ease communication.
items.misconception: Convergence is when two speakers start using identical accents permanently.
items.random_wrong: Convergence means two languages merge into one new language.
items.why: Convergence is a temporary, contextual style shift — not a permanent change. Students confuse it with language change at a community level rather than individual interactional choice.

items.q: What is a speech act according to Searle/Austin?
items.correct: An utterance that performs an action — promising, ordering, apologising, declaring. Speech acts have locutionary (what's said), illocutionary (intended force) and perlocutionary (effect) dimensions.
items.misconception: A speech act is any time someone speaks.
items.random_wrong: A speech act is a performance or a play.
items.why: Speech act theory analyses how language DOES things, not just describes things. 'I now pronounce you married' is a declaration, not a description. Students miss that utterances can be acts in themselves.
Correct answer: Received Pronunciation. “Regional Pronunciation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Vocabulary and grammar. “Pronunciation only” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly what % of UK speak with traditional RP?
items.why: The real figure is 3 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
